content.assessment: Reforming admissions while preserving track structure addresses the SES-independent sorting advantage tied to tutoring access without removing the tracking mechanism itself, which the strongest evidence identifies as an independent driver of inequality. This risks reducing one dimension of inequitable access (who is selected) while leaving the structural mechanism (early selection itself) intact, meaning sorting could be relocated to remaining unregulated channels (private/church schools) rather than reduced overall.
opportunities: Could be paired with accountability review to identify persistent segregation even after admissions redesign
strengths: Directly targets tutoring-sensitive exam content documented as a mechanism of SES-independent sorting advantage
Prior administrative desegregation measures show admissions-level intervention can shift outcomes even without structural abolition
Complementary accountability review mechanism could strengthen monitoring of distributional impact over time
threats: Church-maintained and private schools sit outside central authority, limiting national coverage and creating an escape valve that could relocate rather than reduce sorting
Prior desegregation measures 'stagnated mid-decade,' indicating reform fatigue risk
Divided union positions may prevent unified implementation, weakening fidelity
weaknesses: Leaves intact the early selection age itself, which is independently linked to inequality beyond admissions-mechanism design
Does not resolve the fundamental tracking-driven stratification documented across academic/vocational secondary paths
